title: Best Customer Feedback Tools 2026
description: Find the best customer feedback tools for 2026. We compare Hotjar, Canny, UserVoice, Typeform, and Productboard to help you capture, organize, and act on user insights.
author: TopStackTools Team
content:
Why Customer Feedback Tools Are Non-Negotiable in 2026

The companies that grow consistently in 2026 aren't guessing what customers want — they're listening systematically. Customer feedback tools give product, marketing, and customer success teams a structured way to capture user sentiment, identify friction points, prioritize features, and close the loop with customers who raised issues.

But not all feedback tools serve the same purpose. Some excel at behavioral analytics (watching what users actually do), others at structured feature request management, and others at high-volume survey collection. We tested five of the most widely used options across real SaaS products and service businesses.

Quick Comparison: Which Tool Fits Your Use Case

  • Hotjar: Best for understanding user behavior through heatmaps and session recordings
  • Canny: Best for managing feature requests and customer-driven product roadmaps
  • UserVoice: Best for enterprise teams with structured feedback and product planning workflows
  • Typeform: Best for beautiful, high-completion-rate surveys and form-based feedback
  • Productboard: Best for product teams that need to connect customer feedback directly to roadmap decisions

1. Hotjar — Best for Behavioral User Research

Hotjar is the industry standard for understanding how users actually interact with your product or website — not just what they say about it. Through heatmaps, session recordings, and on-site surveys, Hotjar reveals the gap between stated preferences and real behavior.

Core Features

  • Heatmaps: Click, move, and scroll heatmaps show exactly where users focus attention and where they give up.
  • Session recordings: Watch individual user sessions to identify confusion, rage clicks, and drop-off moments.
  • Feedback widgets: Embed one-click sentiment widgets on any page so users can flag issues in the moment they experience them.
  • Surveys: Trigger on-site surveys based on user behavior — exit intent, time on page, or specific URL visits.

What Could Be Better

Hotjar is primarily a research and observation tool, not a structured feedback management system. It doesn't organize feedback into themes or feed into a product roadmap. You'll need a complementary tool for that.

Pricing

Free plan available with limited recordings. Plus starts at $32/month. Business from $80/month with advanced filters and integrations.

2. Canny — Best for Feature Request Management

Canny solves a specific but critical problem: what do you do with the endless stream of feature requests that come in through support tickets, sales calls, and social media? Canny creates a structured, public-facing board where customers can submit and upvote requests, giving your product team a prioritized signal of what matters most.

Core Features

  • Public feedback boards: Customers submit ideas and vote on existing ones. The upvote count becomes a real prioritization signal.
  • Changelog: When you ship a feature, announce it to the customers who requested it. They get notified automatically.
  • Roadmap view: Share a public or private roadmap that shows the status of requested features.
  • Integrations: Connect to Jira, Linear, GitHub, Slack, and Intercom so feedback flows directly into your development workflow.

Pricing

Free for up to 100 tracked users. Growth plan at $79/month. Business plan at $359/month for larger teams.

3. UserVoice — Best for Enterprise Feedback Programs

UserVoice has been the enterprise feedback standard for over a decade. Its platform is designed for organizations where feedback comes from multiple channels — sales, support, surveys, and direct user submissions — and needs to be aggregated, analyzed, and connected to strategic product decisions.

Core Features

  • Multi-source feedback capture: Collect feedback from support tickets, NPS surveys, sales calls, and direct submissions in one place.
  • Idea prioritization: Score and rank feature ideas by revenue impact, customer segment, and strategic alignment.
  • Executive reporting: Generate stakeholder-ready reports showing which customer segments are asking for what.
  • Smart inbox: Automatically categorize incoming feedback by theme using AI-assisted tagging.

Pricing

UserVoice is enterprise-priced. Contact their team for current pricing — typically starts at several hundred dollars per month for full platform access.

4. Typeform — Best for High-Quality Survey Feedback

Typeform's conversational survey format consistently achieves completion rates 2-3x higher than traditional form tools. If your primary feedback mechanism is surveys — NPS, CSAT, onboarding feedback, or exit surveys — Typeform's design makes the experience enjoyable enough that users actually finish.

Core Features

  • Conversational format: One question at a time keeps users engaged and reduces abandonment.
  • Logic jumps: Show different questions based on previous answers for highly personalized surveys.
  • Integration ecosystem: Connect responses directly to HubSpot, Salesforce, Slack, Notion, and 500+ other tools.
  • Beautiful templates: Hundreds of pre-built survey templates for NPS, product feedback, event registrations, and more.

If you're collecting leads through feedback surveys, consider pairing Typeform with Systeme.io — its built-in email automation lets you follow up with respondents automatically based on their answers, creating a seamless lead nurture flow without extra tools.

Pricing

Basic plan free with limited responses. Plus at $25/month. Business at $83/month. Enterprise pricing available.

5. Productboard — Best for Connecting Feedback to Roadmap

Productboard is purpose-built for product managers who need to go from raw customer feedback to prioritized roadmap decisions. It sits between your feedback collection tools and your development workflow, acting as the intelligent layer that helps you understand what to build next and why.

Core Features

  • Insights repository: Import feedback from Intercom, Zendesk, Salesforce, and other sources. Tag and link insights to specific features.
  • Feature prioritization: Score features using a customizable priority matrix that weights customer impact, effort, and strategic value.
  • Roadmap builder: Create multiple roadmap views for different audiences — engineering, executive, customer-facing.
  • Portal: Share a public feedback portal where customers can submit and vote on ideas.

Pricing

Starter at $19/maker/month. Pro at $59/maker/month. Scale pricing is custom for larger organizations.

Building a Complete Feedback System

The most effective feedback programs don't rely on a single tool. A common setup: Hotjar for behavioral research, Typeform for structured surveys, and Canny or Productboard for managing what to do with the insights. The key is closing the loop — when customers see that their feedback shaped a shipped feature, they become active advocates.

Pair your feedback stack with a solid internal knowledge base in project management tools so feedback themes are visible across the whole team, not just the product manager's inbox.

  • Best Website Analytics Tools 2026
  • Best Social Media Management Tools 2026

title: Best Business Intelligence Tools Small Business 2026
description: The best business intelligence tools for small businesses in 2026. Compare Metabase, Looker, Tableau, Power BI, and Google Data Studio for actionable data insights.
author: TopStackTools Team
content:
Small Businesses Need Data Too

Business intelligence used to require a data engineering team, a six-figure software budget, and months of implementation. In 2026, that's no longer true. Modern BI tools have democratized data analysis to the point where a five-person team can build live dashboards, automated reports, and cross-source data visualizations without a single line of SQL (though the option is there if you want it).

The challenge for small businesses isn't whether to use BI tools — it's choosing the right one. We evaluated five leading platforms across ease of setup, cost, data connectivity, and the quality of insights a non-technical business owner can realistically extract.

Quick Comparison

  • Metabase: Best open-source option — free, self-hosted, and surprisingly powerful
  • Looker: Best for data-mature teams that want governed, reusable metrics
  • Tableau: Best for visual storytelling and complex data exploration
  • Power BI: Best for businesses already in the Microsoft ecosystem
  • Google Data Studio (Looker Studio): Best free option for Google-centric data stacks

1. Metabase — Best Open-Source BI for Small Teams

Metabase is the rare BI tool that genuinely works for non-technical users while remaining powerful enough for analysts. Its open-source version is free to self-host, making it the most cost-effective option for budget-conscious small businesses. The cloud-hosted version starts at a reasonable price with zero infrastructure overhead.

Why Small Businesses Love Metabase

  • Question-based interface: Ask questions of your data in plain language. No SQL required for most common queries.
  • Connect to anything: PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB, BigQuery, Snowflake, CSV uploads — Metabase connects to nearly every data source small businesses use.
  • Dashboards: Build live dashboards and share them with team members or embed them in internal tools.
  • Alerts: Set up automatic email or Slack alerts when key metrics cross a threshold.

What Could Be Better

Self-hosting requires some technical setup. Advanced features like SSO, sandboxing, and audit logs require the paid Pro or Enterprise plan. The UI feels less polished than Tableau or Looker.

Pricing

Open source: free (self-hosted). Cloud Starter: $85/month for up to 5 users. Pro: $500/month.

2. Looker — Best for Governed Metrics at Scale

Looker (now part of Google Cloud) is the enterprise standard for organizations that want a single source of truth for their business metrics. Its LookML modeling layer lets data teams define metrics once and make them available to every stakeholder, ensuring everyone is working from consistent definitions.

Core Features

  • LookML: Define your business logic in a modeling layer so "revenue," "active users," and "churn rate" mean the same thing across every report.
  • Embedded analytics: Embed Looker dashboards directly in your product or internal tools.
  • Looker Studio integration: Connect to Google's free visualization layer for lightweight reporting needs.
  • Scheduling and alerts: Deliver scheduled reports to email, Slack, or webhooks.

Pricing

Looker is priced for mid-market and enterprise. Expect $3,000+/month for typical deployments. May be over-engineered for very small teams.

3. Tableau — Best for Visual Data Exploration

Tableau remains the gold standard for data visualization. When you need to explore a complex dataset visually and create dashboards that genuinely communicate insights to a non-technical audience, Tableau's drag-and-drop interface is unmatched. It connects to virtually any data source and produces publication-quality charts and interactive dashboards.

Core Features

  • Viz library: Dozens of chart types with intelligent suggestions based on your data structure.
  • Tableau Prep: Built-in data cleaning and transformation tool for messy source data.
  • Publish to web: Share interactive dashboards via Tableau Public (free) or Tableau Cloud.
  • AI insights: Tableau's Explain Data feature automatically surfaces statistical drivers behind data points.

Pricing

Tableau Creator at $75/user/month. Viewer licenses at $15/user/month. Tableau Public is free for public data sharing.

4. Microsoft Power BI — Best for Microsoft Shops

If your team uses Microsoft 365, Azure, or SQL Server, Power BI is the obvious choice. The integration depth with the Microsoft ecosystem is unmatched, and the pricing is exceptional — especially since Power BI Desktop is free for individual use and the Pro plan is competitively priced.

Core Features

  • Excel integration: Refresh and analyze Excel data inside Power BI with full relationship modeling.
  • Azure connectivity: Native connectors to Azure Synapse, Azure SQL, Cosmos DB, and the full Azure data stack.
  • DAX: Powerful formula language for creating custom calculated measures and KPIs.
  • Power BI Service: Publish reports to the web and schedule automatic data refreshes.

Power BI's reporting capabilities pair well with revenue tracking — if you're running campaigns through Systeme.io, you can pull sales data directly into Power BI to build unified performance dashboards across your marketing funnel.

Pricing

Desktop: free. Pro: $10/user/month. Premium per user: $20/user/month. Power BI Premium (org-wide): $4,995/month.

5. Google Looker Studio — Best Free Option

Formerly Google Data Studio, Looker Studio is completely free and delivers surprisingly capable dashboards when your data lives in Google's ecosystem. If you run Google Analytics, Google Ads, Google Search Console, and Google Sheets, Looker Studio lets you combine all of that into a single live dashboard with zero cost.

Core Features

  • Free forever: No usage limits, no seat caps, no paid plans required.
  • Native Google connectors: GA4, Google Ads, Search Console, BigQuery, Sheets, and more connect in seconds.
  • Community connectors: Third-party connectors (some free, some paid) extend coverage to Facebook Ads, Shopify, HubSpot, and hundreds of other platforms.
  • Sharing: Share dashboards publicly or with specific Google accounts.

What Could Be Better

Looker Studio lacks advanced data modeling, robust alerting, and the depth of analysis that paid tools offer. Blending data from multiple sources can be slow. Best used as a reporting layer, not a full BI platform.

Which BI Tool Should You Choose?

For most small businesses starting out, the answer is simple: start with Looker Studio if you're Google-centric, or Metabase if you have a database. Both are free, powerful enough for most use cases, and won't lock you into an expensive contract while you figure out what questions you actually need to answer. Once your data needs mature, Tableau or Power BI offer the depth to grow with you.

See how BI tools complement your broader analytics stack in our guide to best website analytics tools.

  • Best Time Tracking Software 2026

title: Best Remote Desktop Software 2026
description: Compare the best remote desktop software for 2026. TeamViewer, AnyDesk, Chrome Remote Desktop, Parsec, and Splashtop tested for speed, security, and ease of use.
author: TopStackTools Team
content:
Remote Access Is Now a Business Essential

Remote desktop software has graduated from an IT niche tool to a core business utility. Whether you're an IT administrator supporting a distributed workforce, a developer accessing a beefy workstation from a laptop, or a creative professional running resource-intensive software remotely, reliable remote access software is no longer optional.

The market has also expanded dramatically. What used to be dominated by a single player (TeamViewer) now has strong competition across every use case and price point. We tested five leading remote desktop tools across connection quality, latency, security, and pricing to help you make the right call.

Quick Comparison

  • TeamViewer: Best for enterprise IT support and established business use
  • AnyDesk: Best for fast connections on limited bandwidth
  • Chrome Remote Desktop: Best free option for basic personal use
  • Parsec: Best for gaming and high-performance creative workloads
  • Splashtop: Best value for business remote access and IT teams

1. TeamViewer — Best for Enterprise IT Support

TeamViewer is the most recognized name in remote desktop software, and for good reason. Its feature set is the most comprehensive available — covering unattended access, attended support sessions, mobile device management, and enterprise-grade security policies. It's the default choice for IT teams supporting large fleets of devices.

Core Features

  • Unattended access: Connect to remote computers without anyone present at the other end. Essential for server management and off-hours maintenance.
  • Multi-platform: Windows, Mac, Linux, iOS, and Android are all supported with native apps.
  • Remote monitoring and management: The TeamViewer RMM add-on turns it into a full IT management platform.
  • Security: End-to-end encryption, two-factor authentication, device whitelisting, and session logging for compliance.

What Could Be Better

TeamViewer's pricing has become aggressively expensive, especially for small businesses. The free version is limited to personal use and frequently triggers commercial-use restrictions. Connection quality can vary compared to lower-latency competitors.

Pricing

Business plan starts at $50.90/month. Premium and Corporate tiers scale up significantly. Enterprise pricing is custom.

2. AnyDesk — Best for Low-Bandwidth Connections

AnyDesk was built from the ground up for speed on constrained connections. Its proprietary DeskRT codec compresses screen data more efficiently than competing tools, delivering smooth remote sessions even on slow or unstable internet connections. It's particularly popular in regions with less reliable infrastructure.

Core Features

  • DeskRT codec: Achieves 60fps remote sessions at low latency, even on connections as slow as 100 Kbps.
  • Lightweight client: The AnyDesk client is under 4MB and requires no installation to run.
  • File transfer: Drag-and-drop file transfer between local and remote machines during a session.
  • Privacy mode: Blank the remote screen during a session so on-site users can't see what the remote operator is doing.

Pricing

Solo plan at $14.90/month. Standard at $29.90/month. Advanced at $79.90/month. Free for personal use.

3. Chrome Remote Desktop — Best Free Option

Chrome Remote Desktop is Google's free, browser-based remote access tool. It requires Google Chrome and a Google account, and it delivers surprisingly capable remote sessions for personal use — accessing your home computer from work, helping a family member with tech support, or reaching files you forgot to transfer. There's nothing to pay and no account beyond Google required.

Core Features

  • Zero cost: Completely free, no subscription required.
  • Instant setup: Install the Chrome extension, generate a PIN, and you're connected in under two minutes.
  • Cross-platform: Access Windows, Mac, or Linux from any Chrome browser or the Chrome Remote Desktop mobile app.
  • Good enough performance: Works well for most personal use cases with decent internet on both ends.

What Could Be Better

Chrome Remote Desktop lacks advanced features — no file transfer UI, no clipboard sync on all platforms, no remote printing, no session recording, and no commercial support options. It's a personal tool, not a business one.

Pricing

Free.

4. Parsec — Best for Gaming and GPU Workloads

Parsec was built for a specific use case: high-performance remote access with near-zero latency and full GPU acceleration. Originally designed for cloud gaming, it's become the go-to tool for creative professionals who want to run GPU-intensive workloads (3D rendering, video editing, VFX) on a powerful remote workstation while working from a lighter laptop.

Core Features

  • 60fps at low latency: Parsec's streaming technology is optimized for interactive sessions where every millisecond matters.
  • Hardware-accelerated encoding: Uses NVIDIA, AMD, or Intel GPU encoding for superior quality at lower bandwidth.
  • Multi-monitor support: Access multi-monitor setups remotely with independent control of each display.
  • Parsec for Teams: Business version adds centralized management, SSO, and audit logs.

Pricing

Free for personal use. Warp (premium personal) at $8/month. Teams plan at $35/month for up to 5 users.

5. Splashtop — Best Value for Business Teams

Splashtop offers enterprise-grade remote access at a price point that genuinely works for small businesses. Its performance rivals TeamViewer in most benchmarks, and its feature set covers the full range of business needs — unattended access, remote support, IT management, and even classroom remote access for education — at a fraction of TeamViewer's cost.

Core Features

  • High-performance streaming: 4K and 60fps support on capable connections for crisp, responsive remote sessions.
  • Unattended access: Set up devices for always-on remote access without requiring someone to accept a connection.
  • Remote support tools: File transfer, remote print, session recording, and multi-monitor navigation all included.
  • Security: TLS and 256-bit AES encryption, device authentication, two-step verification, and IP whitelisting.

For distributed teams that depend on remote access to stay productive, pairing Splashtop with a team communication and project management hub helps maintain visibility across remote workflows. If your business also runs client funnels remotely, Systeme.io keeps sales pipelines and automation running without requiring physical presence.

Pricing

Business Access Solo at $5/month per user. Business Access Pro at $8.25/month per user. Enterprise pricing available.

How to Choose the Right Remote Desktop Tool

The decision comes down to three factors: your use case, your budget, and your infrastructure.

  • IT support teams: TeamViewer (budget permitting) or Splashtop for best value.
  • Individual users on limited budgets: Chrome Remote Desktop is genuinely good enough for basic needs.
  • Creative professionals and developers: Parsec's performance is in a different league for GPU workloads.
  • Teams on slow connections: AnyDesk's codec efficiency gives it a real edge.

  • Best Time Tracking Software 2026
  • Best Website Analytics Tools 2026

title: Best Social Media Scheduling Tools 2026
description: Compare the best social media scheduling tools for 2026. Buffer, Later, Hootsuite, Publer, and Pallyy reviewed for solo creators, agencies, and growing teams.
author: TopStackTools Team
content:
Consistency Is the Engine of Social Media Growth

In 2026, social media success is rarely about a single viral post. It's about showing up consistently, across the right platforms, at the right times, with content that resonates with your audience. That kind of consistency is nearly impossible to maintain manually — which is why social media scheduling tools have become essential for creators, marketers, and business owners alike.

The market has matured significantly. Today's scheduling tools don't just queue up posts — they analyze performance, suggest optimal posting times, manage multi-platform content calendars, and support team collaboration workflows. We tested five of the most popular options to help you find your fit.

Quick Comparison

  • Buffer: Best clean, simple scheduler for individuals and small teams
  • Later: Best visual content calendar for Instagram-first brands
  • Hootsuite: Best for agencies and large teams managing many accounts
  • Publer: Best value with the strongest feature-to-price ratio
  • Pallyy: Best for agencies needing white-label client reporting

1. Buffer — Best for Simplicity and Clean Workflow

Buffer has built its reputation on doing one thing extremely well: making it easy to schedule and publish social media content without friction. Its interface is clean, its learning curve is minimal, and its analytics give you exactly what you need to understand what's working — no more, no less.

Core Features

  • Multi-platform scheduling: Schedule to Instagram, Facebook, X (Twitter), LinkedIn, Pinterest, TikTok, and YouTube in one place.
  • Queue system: Set up posting time slots and fill your queue with content. Buffer automatically fills slots in order.
  • Analytics: See reach, engagement, clicks, and follower growth for each platform and individual post.
  • Start Page: Create a simple link-in-bio landing page to drive social traffic to your most important destinations.

What Could Be Better

Buffer's feature set is intentionally lean — advanced social inbox, competitor analysis, and deep reporting require third-party tools. It's a scheduler, not a full social media management suite.

Pricing

Free plan: 3 channels, 10 scheduled posts each. Essentials: $5/month per channel. Team: $10/month per channel. Agency: $100/month for 10 channels.

2. Later — Best for Visual Content Calendars

Later is the scheduling tool of choice for brands and creators who lead with visual content — particularly on Instagram, Pinterest, and TikTok. Its drag-and-drop visual calendar lets you see exactly how your feed will look before posts go live, which is essential for brands where aesthetic consistency drives engagement.

Core Features

  • Visual Instagram planner: Preview how posts will appear in your grid before scheduling. Drag to reorder.
  • Media library: Upload and organize all your brand assets in Later's cloud library for fast scheduling.
  • Linkin.bio: Later's link page turns Instagram and TikTok profiles into clickable shopping or landing pages.
  • Best time to post: Later analyzes your audience engagement history and recommends optimal posting windows.

What Could Be Better

Later's strength is visual platforms. Its LinkedIn and X support is functional but not a core focus. For text-heavy content strategies, other tools may serve better.

Pricing

Starter: $16.67/month (1 user, 1 social set). Growth: $30/month. Advanced: $53.33/month. Agency: custom.

3. Hootsuite — Best for Agencies and Large Teams

Hootsuite is the most feature-complete social media management platform available, covering scheduling, social listening, engagement, analytics, and team collaboration in a single dashboard. It's built for teams and agencies managing dozens of accounts across multiple clients, and its depth reflects that scale.

Core Features

  • Streams: Monitor multiple social feeds, keywords, mentions, and DMs in a customizable dashboard.
  • Bulk scheduling: Upload and schedule hundreds of posts via CSV for large-volume content calendars.
  • Team permissions: Assign roles, create approval workflows, and manage who can publish to which accounts.
  • Social listening: Track brand mentions, competitor activity, and industry conversations in real time.

What Could Be Better

Hootsuite's pricing has increased substantially. For individuals or small teams, the cost is hard to justify versus lighter alternatives. The interface can feel overwhelming for new users.

Pricing

Professional: $99/month (1 user, 10 accounts). Team: $249/month (3 users, 20 accounts). Enterprise: custom.

4. Publer — Best Feature-to-Price Ratio

Publer consistently delivers more features per dollar than any other scheduling tool in this comparison. It supports all major platforms, includes a full AI writing assistant, offers a visual calendar, link-in-bio page, analytics, and team collaboration — all at pricing that competes with tools offering half the features.

Core Features

  • AI assist: Generate post captions, hashtag suggestions, and content variations with built-in AI.
  • Recycling: Automatically re-queue top-performing evergreen content on a schedule.
  • Watermarking: Add brand watermarks to images automatically before publishing.
  • Multi-account management: Manage unlimited accounts on higher plans — ideal for agencies.

Publer pairs well with a broader content and marketing funnel. If you're using social scheduling to drive leads into your business, connecting your content strategy to a platform like Systeme.io lets you automate follow-up sequences, landing pages, and email nurtures from a single dashboard — turning social traffic into actual revenue.

Pricing

Free plan: 3 accounts, 10 posts each. Professional: $12/month. Business: $21/month. Enterprise: custom.

5. Pallyy — Best for Agencies with Client Reporting

Pallyy is a rising tool that's won serious loyalty among social media agencies. It offers all the standard scheduling features alongside white-label reporting that agencies can brand and deliver directly to clients, team collaboration tools, and a clean interface that makes onboarding new clients fast.

Core Features

  • White-label reports: Generate branded PDF performance reports for clients with your agency's logo and colors.
  • Social inbox: Manage comments and DMs across Instagram, Facebook, and LinkedIn in one unified inbox.
  • Grid planner: Visual Instagram grid preview similar to Later.
  • Team management: Assign posts to team members, set up approval workflows, and manage client access.

Pricing

Free plan available with limited features. Premium: $18/month per social set (unlimited scheduling). Agencies manage multiple social sets on one account.

Choosing the Right Scheduling Tool

The best social media scheduling tool depends entirely on your situation:

  • Individuals and small businesses: Buffer or Publer offer the cleanest experience at the lowest cost.
  • Visual-first brands on Instagram/TikTok: Later's grid planner and media library are hard to beat.
  • Agencies managing many clients: Pallyy's white-label reporting or Hootsuite's depth for larger operations.
  • Teams on tight budgets: Publer's feature set punches well above its price point.

Once you've locked in your scheduling workflow, see how it fits into your broader social strategy with our guide to best social media management tools.
